EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi Valley during Tuesday January 27th. The low then strengthened, and tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley Wednesday morning on January 28th, into Pennsylvania during Wednesday afternoon, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 29th. ||This storm spread a significant wintry mix of precipitation across western Massachusetts, beginning early Wednesday morning, and ending early Thursday morning.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across higher elevations within northern Berkshire County. In addition, some freezing rain occurred, with ice accretion of generally under one tenth of an inch occurring.||The wintry mix led to either closures, or delayed openings of numerous schools and businesses both Wednesday and Thursday mornings. In addition, treacherous travel conditions existed during this period.
Strong low pressure moved over the Cape Cod Canal and coupled with a strong low level jet to produce a burst of heavy snow over much of Southern New England during the morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large branch was downed by the weight of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through southern New England from the southwest spreading snow across the area.  This was followed by strong warm advection resulting in a precipitation change to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five and a half inches of snow fell across western Franklin county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east from the eastern Great Lakes Sunday morning January 18th, across northern New England Sunday afternoon and evening, to a position off the northern New England coast by Monday morning January 19th. ||An area of moderate snowfall accompanied the passage of this system across much of east central New York and adjacent western New England from Sunday morning, into early Monday morning. ||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across north central Berkshire County.||The steadiest snowfall occurred during Sunday morning, before tapering to snow showers and flurries Sunday afternoon and evening. The snow then ended by Monday morning.

Light precipitation developed ahead of an approaching warm front during Sunday evening, January 4th. With surface temperatures initially remaining below freezing, the precipitation began as a mix of sleet and freezing rain across the northern Berkshires. The precipitation then changed to plain rain and drizzle by early Monday morning on January 5th. ||Ice accretion from freezing rain was generally one tenth of an inch or less. However, this light icing led to locally hazardous travel conditions late Sunday evening into early Monday morning.

A cold west to northwest flow of air traversing the Great Lakes, combined with the passage of an upper level disturbance, led to numerous snow showers and embedded heavier snow squalls across portions of the Berkshires during Thursday January 8th. ||Within the most persistent snow showers and squalls, snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with some areas experiencing snowfall rates of between one and two inches per hour. The snow showers and squalls developed during the mid morning hours of Thursday, and tapered off during the early evening hours of Thursday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of Pennsylvania, south of Long Island and past the Cape and Islands sending warm, moist air into Southern New England.  This resulted in very heavy rainfall, particularly across Cape Cod, where six inches of rain fell in less than three hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of rain fell in less than three hours over portions of Cape Cod and southern Plymouth County.  Bourne and southern Plymouth were most affected by this with numerous roadways flooded.  Route 130 in Bourne was flooded with eight inches of water while St. Margaret's Street was inundated with three feet of water.  Buzzard's Bay Drive was flooded, as were several basements and businesses along the street.  Several cars became disabled after becoming stuck in flood waters on roadways in Onset (a village of Bourne).  Two feet of water inundated a Pleasantwood Road in Sandwich.  A portion of Lake Avenue in Wareham was washed out.  A section of Bournedale Road was washed out.  In Plymouth, numerous roads were flooded, including Lunns Way, Pond, Nameloc, Raymond, and Hedges Pond Roads, Windward Lane, and South Wind Drive.  Two cars were stuck in flood waters on Pond Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two lightning strikes at Camp Cachalot, a boy scout camp run by the Narragansett Council.  One of them struck near the dining hall where one of the|Scouts was leaning on a metal serving table.  He received a slight shock and, as a cautionary measure, the rescue squad was contacted.  One of the troop leaders accompanied the boy to the hospital where they were met by the parents - he was checked out, found to be OK, and released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England into an unstable atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms forming along the front.  Some of these storms became severe producing strong, damaging winds and frequent lightning strikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large limb along Route 58 was downed by thunderstorm winds, falling on a car and denting it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England into an unstable atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms forming along the front.  Some of these storms became severe producing strong, damaging winds and frequent lightning strikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Brookfield Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England into an unstable atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms forming along the front.  Some of these storms became severe producing strong, damaging winds and frequent lightning strikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on Stafford Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England into an unstable atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms forming along the front.  Some of these storms became severe producing strong, damaging winds and frequent lightning strikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb on Temple Street was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system moved just southeast of the 40 N/70 W benchmark, spreading snow across much of southern New England.  Snowfall largely fell into the advisory criteria - three to six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell across eastern Plymouth County on average.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system moved just southeast of the 40 N/70 W benchmark, spreading snow across much of southern New England.  Snowfall largely fell into the advisory criteria - three to six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in eastern Norfolk County on average.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air moving rapidly into Southern New England resulted in windy conditions across much of the area.  Sustained wind speeds were mainly in the 30 to 40 mph range with gusts up to around 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Westover Air Force Base (KBAF)near Westfield recorded sustained wind speeds of 32 mph and gusts to 45 mph.  Two trees and two antennas were blown down in the Union Street area of Westfield, leading us to believe there were higher gusts at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving over the Cape Cod Canal spread snow across northwestern Massachusetts and portions of southern New Hampshire.  Most snowfall totals were in the three to five inch range.  The east slopes of the Berkshire mountains in Franklin County received six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to seven inches of snow fell across western Franklin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with a strong low level jet to produce strong winds across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Blue Hill Observatory (KMQE) recorded a wind gust to 59 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast into the eastern Great Lakes by Sunday morning February 22nd, and into the Canadian Maritimes by Monday morning February 23rd. ||A cold front associated with this low approached eastern New York and western New England during Sunday, preceded and accompanied by snow and rain. A period of moderate to locally heavy snowfall occurred across portions of the Berkshires, beginning Sunday morning, and tapering off early Monday morning.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches across lower elevations, especially within southern Berkshire County, to between 6 and 10 inches across higher elevations of northern Berkshire County.

A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast into the eastern Great Lakes by Sunday morning February 22nd, and into the Canadian Maritimes by Monday morning February 23rd. ||A cold front associated with this low approached eastern New York and western New England during Sunday, preceded and accompanied by snow and rain. A period of moderate to locally heavy snowfall occurred across portions of the Berkshires, beginning Sunday morning, and tapering off early Monday morning.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches across lower elevations, especially within southern Berkshire County, to between 6 and 10 inches across higher elevations of northern Berkshire County.
A single isolated thunderstorm developed over Hampden County, producing penny sized hail.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed as the result of an upper level disturbance moving through southern New England.  Unusually cold air in the upper levels aided in the development of large hail within these storms.

Strong low pressure moving through New York State coupled with a vigorous low level jet resulted in periods of damaging wind across Southern New England. Rain and occasional thunder also occurred.  In addition, minor coastal flooding threatened the South Coast of Massachusetts and Rhode Island.  Moderate coastal flooding occurred in coastal Bristol County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at Beverly Municipal Airport (KBVY) recorded sustained wind speeds to 36 mph and gusts to 46 mph.  In addition, reports were received from Lynn and Salem of large branches blown down onto wires, taking the wires down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving through New York State coupled with a vigorous low level jet resulted in periods of damaging wind across Southern New England. Rain and occasional thunder also occurred.  In addition, minor coastal flooding threatened the South Coast of Massachusetts and Rhode Island.  Moderate coastal flooding occurred in coastal Bristol County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, MA (KMQE) recorded sustained wind speeds of 48 mph and gusts to 72 mph.  In addition, a tree was blown down onto a car and a house in Quincy.  Trees and large branches were downed in Braintree and Weymouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving through New York State coupled with a vigorous low level jet resulted in periods of damaging wind across Southern New England. Rain and occasional thunder also occurred.  In addition, minor coastal flooding threatened the South Coast of Massachusetts and Rhode Island.  Moderate coastal flooding occurred in coastal Bristol County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 53 mph wind gust was recorded on a ham radio operators home weather station in Middleboro.  In addition, many trees were downed in Bridgewater, Brockton, Plympton, East Bridgewater, and Lakeville.  Two utility poles were downed in Bridgewater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving through New York State coupled with a vigorous low level jet resulted in periods of damaging wind across Southern New England. Rain and occasional thunder also occurred.  In addition, minor coastal flooding threatened the South Coast of Massachusetts and Rhode Island.  Moderate coastal flooding occurred in coastal Bristol County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 51 mph was recorded on a ham radio operators home weather station in Dartmouth.  In addition, trees were downed in Dartmouth, Warren, Freetown, Fairhaven, and New Bedford.  Several street lights were downed in New Bedford, including one that landed on a house.  Damage to the house is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving through New York State coupled with a vigorous low level jet resulted in periods of damaging wind across Southern New England. Rain and occasional thunder also occurred.  In addition, minor coastal flooding threatened the South Coast of Massachusetts and Rhode Island.  Moderate coastal flooding occurred in coastal Bristol County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Dartmouth: Rocks and other ocean debris were found on the side of Smith Neck Road.  In addition, oceanwater reached a house with water in neighboring yards on Water Street.  The ocean was up to the level of the sea wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the mid-Atlantic coast intensified dramatically resulting in widespread snowfall along the northeast corridor of the U.S.  While the mid-Atlantic received much of the snow and wind from this storm, snow spread across much of Southern New England and blizzard conditions occurred in Newport, Rhode Island and Marshfield, Massachusetts.  Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 3 inches in northwestern Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire to 18 to 20 inches across Rhode Island and southeastern Massachusetts.  This resulted in numerous flight cancellations out of T.F. Green Airport in Providence and Logan Airport in Boston, school closings, and a struggle by plows to keep the roads clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to twenty one inches of snow fell across Cape Cod.  Strong winds also affected the Cape with a sustained wind speed of 44 mph recorded at the AWOS at Provincetown and a 62 mph wind gust recorded on a spotters home weather station.  Several trees and large branches were downed by the combination of high winds and heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to the southeast of Nantucket bringing rain, a little bit of snow, and wind to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees in Plymouth were downed.  Some of these trees landed on wires, one on a car.  No injuries were reported.  Trees were also downed in Scituate and Marshfield.  One of the trees downed in Marshfield landed on wires, which put enough pressure on the utility pole that it came down too.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to the southeast of Nantucket bringing rain, a little bit of snow, and wind to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in Yarmouth, Barnstable, Mashpee, and Bourne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to the southeast of Nantucket bringing rain, a little bit of snow, and wind to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Nantucket Memorial Airport (KACK) recorded sustained wind speeds of 41 mph and gusts to 54 mph.  In addition, a twelve inch diameter tree was downed, blocking Atlantic Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to the southeast of Nantucket bringing rain, a little bit of snow, and wind to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of both Winthrop Parkway and Winthrop Shore Drive were flooded with ocean waters and debris.  A nearby secondary road was closed due to the amount of debris in the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to the southeast of Nantucket bringing rain, a little bit of snow, and wind to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A parking lot at Scituate Harbor was flooded with six to twelve inches of ocean water.  Medium size rocks from the sea were washed into the parking lot.  Lighthouse Road was flooded with six inches of water and splashover blocked access to Lighthouse Point.  Water splashing over the sea wall was reported to be hitting the roofs of nearby homes.  In addition, the parking lot for Burts Restaurant on Route 3A in Plymouth was flooded with water splashing over the sea wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to the southeast of Nantucket bringing rain, a little bit of snow, and wind to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seaview Avenue from Oak Bluffs to Harthaven were flooded with water and debris splashing over the seawall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to the southeast of Nantucket bringing rain, a little bit of snow, and wind to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington and Lafayette Streets were flooded with six inches of seawater.  Several streets near Brant Point were flooded as well.  The parking lots at Jetty Beach and Children's Beach also were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system originating over Texas moved northeast and across the central and eastern Great Lakes Wednesday, December 9th. A secondary low formed over the mid Atlantic region early Wednesday morning and strengthened as it moved northeast across Long Island and southern New England during the day. ||Snow overspread the Berkshires between 4 AM and 5 AM LST Wednesday morning. The snow was heavy at times with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour |in some locations. Some warmer air worked in aloft causing sleet to mix with the snow during the day Wednesday.  The precipitation became light and tapered off by early Wednesday afternoon as it changed to rain below 1000 feet, and freezing rain above 1000 feet in elevation. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches up to almost 14 inches.

The snow was heavy at times with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour |in some locations. Some warmer air worked in aloft causing sleet to mix with the snow during the day Wednesday.  The precipitation became light and tapered off by early Wednesday afternoon as it changed to rain below 1000 feet, and freezing rain above 1000 feet in elevation. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches up to almost 14 inches.

A large and powerful low pressure system over the upper midwest moved slowly northeastward as it weakened on Saturday, December 26th. In the meantime, a secondary low had formed over the mid atlantic region and moved gradually northeast. A light wintry mix of precipitation overspread the Berkshires late Christmas night and continued well into Saturday night, causing travel to be treacherous at times.  ||Snow and sleet accumulations of up to 2 inches occurred, along with ice accretions of up to one tenth of an inch.

A low pressure system and an upper level disturbance moved over the region Monday, December 28th, resulting in a light to moderate snowfall. A tight pressure gradient developed Tuesday morning, December 29th, as the low strengthened and headed northeast, while high pressure built in from the west. Strong northwest winds with gusts of 30 to 50 mph led to blowing and drifting of the snow.

The combination of gusty winds, and low temperatures produced wind chills of -15 to -20 F across the higher elevations of western Massachusetts early Thursday morning on January 1st.

A strong low pressure system moved through southern New England from the southwest spreading snow across the area.  This was followed by strong warm advection resulting in a precipitation change to freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A little over four inches of snow fell across southern Worcester County.  This was followed by two tenths of an inch of freezing rain.  This ice accumulation was enough to down a few power lines in Southborough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Ohio Valley and into southern New England. This low developed a secondary low off the coast of New Jersey.  These two low pressure systems resulted in precipitation overspreading the area while warm air moved in aloft.  This made precipitation type a challenge with snow, freezing rain, and sleet occurring across southern New England.  Temperatures rose above freezing for a period during the afternoon allowing some of the precipitation to melt and coat roads with standing water.  Freezing temperatures overnight allowed the water to freeze creating sheets of ice on the roads.  Portions of Interstates 495 and 95 were closed after numerous spinouts occurred.  No serious injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires in Hopkinton and Billerica were downed by a quarter of an inch of ice that fell following two inches of snow.

This resulted in numerous flight cancellations out of T.F. Green Airport in Providence and Logan Airport in Boston, school closings, and a struggle by plows to keep the roads clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to twelve inches of snow fell across southeast Middlesex County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low pressure system tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday January 10th, into central Pennsylvania Saturday night, and then east to a position off the New England coast by Sunday morning, January 11th.||This low pressure system spread moderate to locally heavy snowfall across the Berkshires, beginning early Saturday evening, and ending Sunday morning.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across northeast portions of Berkshire County.

A developing low pressure system tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday January 10th, into central Pennsylvania Saturday night, and then east to a position off the New England coast by Sunday morning, January 11th.||This low pressure system spread moderate to locally heavy snowfall across the Berkshires, beginning early Saturday evening, and ending Sunday morning.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across northeast portions of Berkshire County.

A bitterly cold air mass spread across much of east central New York and adjacent western New England during Friday January 16th. ||Widespread subzero temperatures occurred across western Massachusetts Friday morning, with temperatures as low as -13 F recorded at Clarksburg, -9 F recorded at the North Adams Airport, and -7 F recorded at the Pittsfield Airport.||In addition, some wind added to the extreme cold, with wind chills of |-15 to -24 F.

A bitterly cold air mass spread across much of east central New York and adjacent western New England during Friday January 16th. ||Widespread subzero temperatures occurred across western Massachusetts Friday morning, with temperatures as low as -13 F recorded at Clarksburg, -9 F recorded at the North Adams Airport, and -7 F recorded at the Pittsfield Airport.||In addition, some wind added to the extreme cold, with wind chills of |-15 to -24 F.

A low pressure system tracked from the lower Ohio Valley on Tuesday January 6th, into the eastern Great Lakes during Wednesday January 7th, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 8th. A second low pressure system developed near Long Island during Wednesday, and tracked northeast along the northern New England coast by Thursday morning.||This combination of low pressure systems spread a swath of moderate to heavy precipitation across eastern New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending early Thursday morning.||The precipitation fell as a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the region, before gradually changing to plain rain from south to north during Wednesday as milder air approached from the south. As colder air wrapped back into the region, the precipitation changed to snow before ending later Wednesday night and early Thursday morning.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across higher elevations within eastern Berkshire County. In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain generally ranged from one tenth, to up to one quarter of an inch.||This wintry mix led to widespread hazardous travel conditions during Wednesday morning. In addition, numerous closures, or delayed openings of schools and businesses occurred Wednesday morning.

A coastal low pressure system moved southeast of Nantucket, spreading snow across Southern New England. Snowfall amounts ranged from three inches on Cape Cod to almost twelve inches in southern New Hampshire. This late season storm affected most of the east coast and resulted in hundreds of flight cancellations at Boston's Logan Airport and many car accidents. In Massachusetts, snowfall amounts ranged from three to ten inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Plymouth County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast from the mid Mississippi Valley Wednesday February 18th, into the lower Saint Lawrence Valley by Thursday morning February 19th. An occluded front associated with this low pressure system swept northeast across western New England early Thursday morning, preceded and accompanied by moderate to locally heavy wet snow. ||The heaviest snowfall amounts occurred across higher elevations within  northern Berkshire County, where 3 to 7 inches fell. ||The snow began Wednesday afternoon, and tapered off early Thursday morning.

A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast from the mid Mississippi Valley Wednesday February 18th, into the lower Saint Lawrence Valley by Thursday morning February 19th. An occluded front associated with this low pressure system swept northeast across western New England early Thursday morning, preceded and accompanied by moderate to locally heavy wet snow. ||The heaviest snowfall amounts occurred across higher elevations within  northern Berkshire County, where 3 to 7 inches fell. ||The snow began Wednesday afternoon, and tapered off early Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bus carrying members of the Albany River Rats minor league hockey team rolled on its side on westbound Interstate 90 near Becket early Thursday morning, resulting in three serious injuries.  The bus struck a guard rail and rolled onto its side at approximately 0335 LST.||Slippery roadways from wet snow were believed to be a major factor in this incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong westerly wind flow, and a cold, moist and unstable air mass in place led to numerous snow showers and locally heavier snow squalls across the northern Berkshires.||Snowfall amounts varied from 2 to 4 inches across lower elevations, to 4 to 8 inches across the higher, west facing elevations. There were isolated amounts of up to 10 inches reported at Alford and Savoy where snow squalls were more persistent.||The snow showers and embedded squalls developed late Thursday morning, and tapered off Thursday evening.

An unseasonably cold air mass produced freezing temperatures across much of western Massachusetts during the early morning hours of Tuesday May 19th. The growing season already started in this area after a stretch of unusually warm temperatures during April.

A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast from the southeast coast on Sunday morning, March 1st, to just southeast of Long Island by Monday morning March 2nd. The low then headed northeast into the Canadian maritimes by Tuesday March 3rd.||This storm system spread a swath of moderate to heavy snow across Berkshire County in western Massachusetts, beginning early Monday morning, and ending by late Monday afternoon.||Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches across southern Berkshire County, and 3 to 6 inches across northern Berkshire County. This moderate to heavy snowfall resulted in either closures, or delayed openings of numerous schools and businesses Monday morning, and also created treacherous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute.

A strong cold front moved through Southern New England, combining with strong shear and marginal instability to produce showers and thunderstorms across the area.  These thunderstorms produced some small hail and severe winds, downing trees and branches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on Littleton and Jackson Roads in Ayer were downed by thunderstorm winds.  In addition, large limbs and branches in Groton were downed.

The combination of a passing upper level trough, and unusually cold air in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere, led to the development of numerous thunderstorms across the Berkshires of western Massachusetts during the afternoon and early evening hours on Monday June 15th, many of which contained large quantities of hail. A portion of Route 102 near Stockbridge was closed Monday evening due to approximately 6 inches of hail accumulation, and snowplows were used to clear the hail.||In addition, some thunderstorms were slow moving, and produced locally very intense rainfall rates. This led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported approximately 2 miles east of Pittsfield during a thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in several locations in Franklin County including: On Silver Land and Old Amherst Road in Sunderland, on Bald Mountain Road in Bernardston, and on Route 78 in Warwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Southern New England as an upper level low moved across the area.  Weak low level winds resulted in these storms moving very slowly, if at all.  Rainfall rates of up to four inches per hour produced flash flooding, resulting in street closures and basement flooding at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Morse Street was downed onto power lines by thunderstorm winds.  In addition, tree limbs were downed on Swift Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong coastal storm moved southeast of Nantucket, resulting in coastal flooding and strong winds across portions of coastal Massachusetts.  Because of coastal erosion that began in 2007, the last 5 cottages of First Village (Chatham) on Nauset Beach, were lost in this storm.  During this storm, roughly 40 feet of beach in front of the properties was eroded.  Two of the five cottages had tilted over, with waves lapping at the buildings.  The owners of the remaining cottages asked permission from the town to demolish their buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding occurred in Wellfleet, where a car got stuck in water moving over Lieutenant Island Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Danny formed just east of the Bahamas on August 26, 2009.  Danny remained a tropical storm throughout its travels up the east coast of the United States, then turning extratropical as it passed just east of Cape Cod and the islands August 29 and 30.  Tropical moisture feeding into the area resulted in heavy rainfall mainly across coastal Massachusetts and Rhode Island which led to minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell across Bristol County, resulting in minor street flooding.  In Dartmouth, Russell Mills and Horseneck Roads were flooded and impassable and Old Westport Road was flooded with six inches of water.  River Road near Sylvia Street in New Bedford was closed due to flooding.  Stafford Square in Fall River was flooded with water two and a half feet deep and the westbound lane of Roebson Street was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Bill passed well to the east of Nantucket on August 22-23. While Bill passed Southern New England well offshore, he still made quite an impression on the area. Tropical moisture feeding into the area resulted in heavy rainfall which led to flooding and flash flooding. High swells churned up by Bill resulted in high surf and coastal flooding along portions of the Southern New England shoreline. On Nantucket, minor coastal flooding affected Atlantic Street running through Codfish Park on the southeastern coast of Nantucket.  On the southwestern coast of Nantucket, in Madaket, three homes on Massachusetts Avenue were surrounded by ocean waters.  Only one of the homes is occupied and water two and a half feet deep surrounded that home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding affected Atlantic Street running through Codfish Park on the southeastern coast of Nantucket.  On the southwestern coast of Nantucket, in Madaket, three homes on Massachusetts Avenue were surrounded by ocean waters.  Only one of the homes is occupied and water two and a half feet deep surrounded that home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England bringing cold air in at the upper levels and producing showers and thunderstorms. The cold air at the upper levels created conditions conducive to the production of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded and subsequently closed across much of southern Worcester County.  The worst flooding was experienced in Westborough and Worcester.  The Westborough News reported it as the worst flooding Westborough has seen in years.  The Westborough fire department took 57 calls due to either flooded basements or motorists stranded in flood waters.  Flooding was especially bad in the streets behind Westborough High School, south of the Westborough Reservoir and around the Jackstraw Brook.  In Worcester, numerous streets were flooded and washed out.  Two people were rescued from flood waters in Southborough.  In addition, a couple of propane tanks had to be secured at a house on Parkerville Road because they were floating in the waters.  The Southborough Fire Department received 26 calls of basement flooding.  Six inches of water inundated the Southborough Public Library's children's section, damaging books.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Substantial low level moisture combined with an upper level disturbance to produce showers and thunderstorms across Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were downed by thunderstorm winds, including one on Willis Street that fell on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong coastal storm of tropical origin, moved southeast of Nantucket, resulting in heavy rain, flooding and strong winds across portions of Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, power lines, and branches were downed by strong winds in Westport, Somerset, and Dartmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong coastal storm of tropical origin, moved southeast of Nantucket, resulting in heavy rain, flooding and strong winds across portions of Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lincoln and Cambridge Streets in Worcester were flooded with one to two feet of water.  The intersection of Ward Street and Route 122 in Millbury was flooded with one and a half feet of water.  In Grafton, Hayes Pond overflowed onto East Road spilling water onto the road up to four inches in depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across the region during the afternoon hours and triggered isolated thunderstorms over Berkshire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Street, Route 7 and 20,  in Pittsfield at the Lenox line was closed due to high water. A person was trapped in a vehicle in water estimated at a depth of 12 to 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front stretched from Hartford, Connecticut across northern Rhode Island to southeast Massachusetts combined with an upper level disturbance to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. Plenty of moisture was in place across these areas, increasing the chance for heavy rain and flooding.  A 41 year old man was killed when he was struck by lightning while digging for clams in the tidal flats of Little Pleasant Bay near Orleans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 41 year old man was out digging for quahogs (clams) on the tidal flats of Little Pleasant Bay, a quarter mile east of Sipsons Island when he was struck by lightning and killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through Southern New England coupled with a 50 mph low level jet produced strong gradient winds across much of the area.  Winds remained below advisory criteria (31 mph sustained or 46 mph gusts) but with some trees still partially to fully leafed, these winds did produce some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Logan International Airport (KBOS) recorded sustained wind speeds up to 36 mph and gusts to 47 mph.  Several reports of trees and limbs downed were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across region Saturday, October 24th, bringing a widespread heavy rainfall to the area. Two to 3 inches of rain was reported across Berkshire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 7 in Williamstowm was reported closed due to water from the Broad Brook flowing over a bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England resulting in showers and gusty westerly winds.  In a few locations these winds reached high wind criteria (40 mph sustained or 58 mph gusts).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Worcester Regional Airport (KORH) recorded sustained wind speeds of 35 mph and gusts to 47 mph.  A tree was blown down in Southboro, blocking Route 9 West and Interstate 495.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought rain and warm temperatures to much of Southern New England before a cold front swept through during the late evening/overnight hours bringing gusty winds but an end to the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Weather Observing System at the Cape Cod Coast Guard Air Station (KFMH) recorded sustained winds to 31 mph.  In addition, spotters across Barnstable county reported wind gusts up to 60 mph.  Reports of damage included: large branches downed in Sandwich and Falmouth and trees down in Bourne and Sandwich.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought rain and warm temperatures to much of Southern New England before a cold front swept through during the late evening/overnight hours bringing gusty winds but an end to the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at New Bedford Regional Airport (KEWB) recorded sustained wind speeds up to 32 mph and gusts to 47 mph.  In addition, the Narragansett Bay PORTS system at Fall River recorded sustained wind speeds to 35 mph and a wind gust to 56 mph.  Reports of damage include: large branches and trees downed in New Bedford, Somerset, Westport, Fairhaven, and Dartmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought rain and warm temperatures to much of Southern New England before a cold front swept through during the late evening/overnight hours bringing gusty winds but an end to the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Plymouth Municipal Airport (KPYM) recorded sustained wind speeds of 32 mph with gusts to 46 mph.  In addition, spotters reported wind gusts up to 58 mph.  Trees in Duxbury were downed by these winds.

Based on damage reports in the area, occasional gusts to 58 mph are believed to have occurred throughout coastal Bristol County.  Some of these reports include: large branches and trees down in Fairhaven, Westport, Somerset, New Bedford, and Fall River.

Some of these reports include: a tree eighteen inches in diameter was downed in Topsfield blocking the intersection of Prospect Street and River Road.  In Haverhill, a tree and wires were downed onto a car.

Based on damage reports in the area, occasional gusts to 58 mph are believed to have occurred throughout eastern Norfolk County.  Some of these reports include: trees down in Hingham.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees throughout Taunton, Mansfield, and Raynham were downed by strong winds behind a cold front.  In addition, a telephone pole on Randall Street in Easton was snapped in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England resulting in showers and gusty westerly winds.  In a few locations these winds reached high wind criteria (40 mph sustained or 58 mph gusts).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slow moving cold front approaching from the west, and an abundantly moist and warm air mass in place, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during Wednesday afternoon and evening on July 29th.||Some of the thunderstorms moved slowly, and trained across the same areas for several hours, producing torrential rainfall and resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from training thunderstorms produced flash flooding near Williamstown. Seven to nine inches of flowing water was reported at the intersection of Route 7 and Bridges Road, as Broad Brook overflowed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front stretched south of Long Island moved northward, combining with an upper level disturbance to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. Plenty of moisture was in place across these areas, increasing the chance for heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on North Road was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability increased ahead of an approaching cold front, resulting in showers and thunderstorms across Southern New England.  A few of these storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell on Bankroft Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms.  Warm, moist air and a strong low level jet aided in the development of these storms and several storms became severe, producing hail up to two inches in diameter.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed as the result of an upper level disturbance moving through southern New England.  Unusually cold air in the upper levels aided in the development of large hail within these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to golf ball size hail fell in Hopkinton.  So much hail fell that two hours after the storm moved out of the area, hail was still piled three inches deep in numerous locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist environment with dewpoints in the low 70s and plenty of sunshine combined to produce showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees south of Route 143 were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England bringing cold air in at the upper levels and producing showers and thunderstorms. The cold air at the upper levels created conditions conducive to the production of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell in a swath from southwest of Huntington to north of Huntington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Southern New England as an upper level low moved across the area.  Weak low level winds resulted in these storms moving very slowly, if at all.  Rainfall rates of up to four inches per hour produced flash flooding, resulting in street closures and basement flooding at several locations.

The combination of a passing upper level trough, and unusually cold air in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere, led to the development of numerous thunderstorms across the Berkshires of western Massachusetts during the afternoon and early evening hours on Monday June 15th, many of which contained large quantities of hail. A portion of Route 102 near Stockbridge was closed Monday evening due to approximately 6 inches of hail accumulation, and snowplows were used to clear the hail.||In addition, some thunderstorms were slow moving, and produced locally very intense rainfall rates. This led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense rainfall during several clusters of thunderstorms produced flash flooding in Pittsfield, as well as in Stockbridge and Sandisfield.||In Pittsfield, several roads were closed due to several feet of standing water. At New York Avenue, off Merrill Road, several cars were submerged, and city firefighters and police officers waded through chest high water to rescue trapped motorists.  A school bus also reportedly stalled in flood waters. A portion of North Street was also closed to due rushing water. In addition, a portion of Tyler Street was closed near Grove and Brown streets due to several feet of standing water. The Berkshire Medical Center in Pittsfield also experienced some flooding in a utility room.||In Stockbridge, a portion of Route 183 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low and pool of unusually cold air in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere moved over the region Tuesday, July 7th, leading to the development of thunderstorms across western Massachusetts. Severe thunderstorms occured during the early afternoon and again early in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Washington during a thunderstorm.

Severe thunderstorms occured during the early afternoon and again early in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires were reported down in Richmond due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system approached the region Thursday, July 16th. A warm front associated with the system moved to the northeast by early afternoon placing the local area in a very unstable airmass. A cold front then moved across the area Thursday night. In addition, an upper level low was approaching the Great Lakes Region. A severe thunderstorm moved across the Berkshires Thursday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Dalton during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, humid and unstable airmass was in place as weakening cold front moved across the area Monday afternoon. In addition, a strong upper level disturbance moved over the region, helping to trigger thunderstorms across the Berkshires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A billboard was reported blown over and a picnic table was reported blown 100 feet in Pittsfield due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moved eastward along a stalled frontal boundary across the area and a strong upper level disturbance passed over region aiding in the development of showers and thunderstorms across Berkshire County during Friday afternoon, July 31st. The airmass was quite humid and many storms produced very heavy downpours. In addition, the storms were slow movers. ||Generally, 1 1/4 to 1 1/2 inches of rain was reported across the northeast portion of Berkshire County. This area received heavy rainfall the couple of days prior and the ground was waterlogged, with rivers and streams were already running high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A washout was reported on West Road in Richmond due to very heavy rainfall.
